text: This tree right behind me is actually where cinnamon comes from, so today we're gonna harvest some. Over the past year, so this tree has grown a lot, so it's time to trim it. After trimming the tree, I was left with quite a few good-sized branches. The cinnamon that we know and love is actually just the dried out inner bark from the cinnamon tree, so if you look closely, you can actually see that there's a dark ring around the branch. To get to that inner bark, the first thing you want to do is go ahead and use a vegetable peeler to remove the outer bark. After that, if I'm using a larger log like this one right here that has a branch coming off the side, I like to make a cut all the way around it, that way I know where to stop for my cinnamon stick, and then I just make a cut from the top to the bottom. After that, I like to give it a good tap all the way around because this helps to loosen that inner bark. After that's done, I go back to that center line that I cut earlier and carefully use my knife to peel off the inner bark layer. The larger the branches, the easier it is to get it all off in one piece. Then I just go ahead and lay all the bark out on a cookie sheet so that it can dry overnight. The next morning, after it's dried out, you can see that it curls up and becomes the cinnamon sticks that we know and love. Then for any of the smaller or broken cinnamon sticks, what I like to do is roughly chop them that way they fit inside of a coffee grinder and then grind them to make ground cinnamon. Once that's done, it should look something like this. Then you can go ahead and sift it and just like that, we have cinnamon sticks and ground cinnamon straight from the garden. So if you ever wondered how cinnamon's made, now you know. Until next time, get outside and see what you can find.
